title: Psat Pstory

jokes.text: It's cool, max out, Hil, they won't be home for hours.
jokes.setup: Will confidently assures Hilary their parents won't be home for hours
jokes.punchline: Immediately followed by 'They're here. Nighty-night.'
jokes.explanation: Classic sitcom misdirection with perfect timing. The immediate contradiction creates a strong comedic beat.

jokes.text: They're here. Nighty-night.
jokes.setup: Will's confident statement about having hours
jokes.punchline: Instant panic and retreat
jokes.explanation: Perfect payoff to the setup. Economy of language and physical comedy implied in the quick escape.

jokes.text: A butler having to do the baking? I'm sorry, it's just a butler having to cook... is rather like a butler having to clean.
jokes.setup: Geoffrey doing cooking duties
jokes.punchline: Cecil's condescending comparison implying Geoffrey does menial work
jokes.explanation: Character-driven humor based on butler class distinctions and professional rivalry.

jokes.text: Yo, what's up, the butler posse?
jokes.setup: Two proper English butlers having tea
jokes.punchline: Will's street greeting treating them like a gang
jokes.explanation: Perfect culture clash humor. Will's street vernacular applied to formal butler scene creates strong character comedy.

jokes.text: Yo, C, you looking buffed, man. What, you been pumping iron?
jokes.setup: Will treating the formal butler like a gym buddy
jokes.punchline: Street slang applied to elderly British butler
jokes.explanation: Continues the culture clash theme with Will's street attitude toward prim butler.

jokes.text: I do what I can, homes.
jokes.setup: Formal British butler
jokes.punchline: Responds with American street slang
jokes.explanation: Unexpected character reversal - the uptight butler adopting street language creates good contrast humor.

jokes.text: No, that's all right, I'm driving later, man. Y'all go ahead and get busy without me.
jokes.setup: Invitation to tea and crumpets
jokes.punchline: Will treating it like drug use invitation
jokes.explanation: Clever misdirection treating innocent tea time as drug activity.

jokes.text: None of that sniffing furniture polish, all right? Just say no.
jokes.setup: Previous drug metaphor
jokes.punchline: Extending the metaphor to furniture polish with Nancy Reagan reference
jokes.explanation: Extends the drug metaphor with period-appropriate 'Just Say No' reference.

jokes.text: At least I don't have to follow him around with a pooper-scooper.
jokes.setup: Discussing the Crandolfs having poodles
jokes.punchline: Geoffrey's undignified task
jokes.explanation: Simple visual humor about butler dignity vs. reality.

jokes.text: You mean those foaming bath tablets they gave you last Christmas?
jokes.setup: Geoffrey bragging about his Jacuzzi
jokes.punchline: Cecil revealing it's just bath tablets
jokes.explanation: Good deflation of Geoffrey's pretensions with specific detail that makes it believable.

jokes.text: They're quite exhilarating if you sit directly on them.
jokes.setup: Bath tablets as 'Jacuzzi'
jokes.punchline: Geoffrey's desperate attempt to maintain dignity
jokes.explanation: Geoffrey's pathetic attempt to save face creates good character humor.

jokes.text: Hey, yo, G. Can I borrow your new TV, man? -Certainly, Master William, it's in my suite. -No, it ain't, G. It's right here.
jokes.setup: Geoffrey bragging about his new TV in his suite
jokes.punchline: Will revealing the tiny TV Geoffrey is actually referring to
jokes.explanation: Perfect deflation of Geoffrey's pretensions with visual payoff. The audience sees the pathetically small TV.

jokes.text: Buy a vowel, you moron. Buy a vowel!
jokes.setup: Will watching game show on tiny TV
jokes.punchline: Will's intense involvement in Wheel of Fortune
jokes.explanation: Character moment showing Will's engagement with TV, mildly funny.

jokes.text: Yeah, and if I were you I'd get a red nose... and a set of big floppy shoes, and call it a day.
jokes.setup: Carlton's condescending advice about PSATs
jokes.punchline: Will calling Carlton a clown
jokes.explanation: Good insult with specific imagery. Clever way to call someone a clown without saying it directly.

jokes.text: Laugh now, but in 10 years I'll be a successful lawyer... and you'll be asking people if they want extra ketchup.
jokes.setup: Will's mockery
jokes.punchline: Carlton's fast food worker prediction
jokes.explanation: Typical Carlton pretension and condescension. The fast food reference is predictable but fits character.

jokes.text: Yeah, right, and in kindergarten they told me... it didn't make a difference where you put the round peg. Next thing I knew I was in the back of the class with a guy who ate paste.
jokes.setup: Carlton's claim you can't study for aptitude tests
jokes.punchline: Will's kindergarten experience proving otherwise
jokes.explanation: Clever analogy using childhood experience. The paste-eater detail adds good specificity and humor.

jokes.text: Boy, do I have a lot of work to do this weekend.
jokes.setup: Will just said what kind of fool works night and day
jokes.punchline: Uncle Phil immediately entering saying he has lots of work
jokes.explanation: Classic sitcom timing joke - character unwittingly contradicts what was just said.

jokes.text: Like I was saying, Carlton, a wise man knows the value of hard work.
jokes.setup: Will just mocked working night and day
jokes.punchline: Will immediately switching sides when Uncle Phil appears
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment showing Will's opportunistic nature. Quick thinking to avoid trouble.

jokes.text: You're gonna get a gold star on your chart right next to 'put away toys'... and 'stay dry through the night.'
jokes.setup: Carlton excited about shaving 7 seconds off his practice test
jokes.punchline: Will treating him like a toddler with a behavior chart
jokes.explanation: Good escalation from gold star to increasingly infantilizing activities. The potty training reference is the perfect cap.

jokes.text: Madam, sir... I have just taken the liberty of rotating your tires... re-grouting the pool... and drawing up plans for a small gazebo.
jokes.setup: Discussion of Geoffrey's raise
jokes.punchline: Geoffrey's over-the-top work performance right on cue
jokes.explanation: Perfect timing and escalation. Geoffrey's transparent attempt to impress with increasingly elaborate tasks.

jokes.text: I think he just brought it up.
jokes.setup: Aunt Viv saying they won't bring up Geoffrey's raise until he does
jokes.punchline: Phil's dry observation after Geoffrey's obvious performance
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective deadpan response to the obvious situation.

jokes.text: Irritate: from the Latin, irritare, to excite or stimulate.
jokes.setup: Being told he's irritating
jokes.punchline: More etymology, proving the point
jokes.explanation: Continues the pattern, showing Carlton can't help himself even when it proves their point.

jokes.text: Shut up: from the big guy, your father.
jokes.setup: Carlton's Latin etymology pattern
jokes.punchline: Uncle Phil breaking the pattern with 'big guy' etymology
jokes.explanation: Perfect payoff to the running gag. Uncle Phil plays along with the format but asserts parental authority. Great structure and timing.

jokes.text: Will dancing/stretching before the test
jokes.setup: Carlton asking if this is how Will prepares for a test
jokes.punchline: Will's unconventional physical preparation
jokes.explanation: Visual gag showing Will's street approach to test prep vs Carlton's academic approach.

jokes.text: See, what it was is we're gonna be sitting down for a lot of hours... so I just thought I'd do something to get my blood flowing.
jokes.setup: Will's dancing/stretching
jokes.punchline: Will's actually logical explanation
jokes.explanation: Subverts expectation by having Will's bizarre behavior actually make sense.

jokes.text: I want to get there early so I can get my pick of the pencils.
jokes.setup: Carlton's test preparation obsession
jokes.punchline: Caring about which pencils to use
jokes.explanation: Perfect Carlton obsessiveness - caring about details that don't matter. Good character consistency.

jokes.text: Or you could throw it up in the air and cover that big hole in the ozone.
jokes.setup: Uncle Phil's oversized Princeton sweatshirt being given to Carlton
jokes.punchline: Will mocking its enormous size with environmental reference
jokes.explanation: Good visual joke about Uncle Phil's size with topical environmental reference. Well-constructed insult.

jokes.text: I'm sorry? I thought you said $20 per week.
jokes.setup: Uncle Phil offering $20 weekly raise
jokes.punchline: Geoffrey's polite disbelief at the insulting amount
jokes.explanation: Geoffrey's polite but clear rejection of the insulting raise offer.

jokes.text: And with my newfound wealth... I can treat myself to a Happy Meal.
jokes.setup: Geoffrey sarcastically thanking for the $20 raise
jokes.punchline: The pathetic luxury he can now afford
jokes.explanation: Perfect sarcasm showing how insulting the raise is. The Happy Meal reference is perfectly chosen for its cheapness.

jokes.text: Who's the most handsome guy in the world?
jokes.setup: Will demanding Carlton answer to get test scores
jokes.punchline: Making Carlton boost his ego first
jokes.explanation: Classic sibling power play - Will making Carlton pay to see his own scores.

jokes.text: And who's the biggest weenie on the planet for $400?
jokes.setup: Continuing to make Carlton degrade himself
jokes.punchline: Jeopardy format for humiliation
jokes.explanation: Continues the humiliation with game show format. The $400 reference adds specificity.

jokes.text: Yes! Mom! Dad! I got a 114 on the PSATs. I'm smart. I'm brilliant. I'm the most intelligent person who ever lived.
jokes.setup: Carlton finally getting validation
jokes.punchline: Escalating from smart to most intelligent person ever
jokes.explanation: Carlton's characteristic over-the-top reaction to success. The escalation fits his personality.

jokes.text: A 91 is... a very good reason why you should take this test again, Will.
jokes.setup: Will's 91 score
jokes.punchline: Uncle Phil's diplomatic way of saying it's terrible
jokes.explanation: Perfect Uncle Phil diplomacy - finding the politest way to say Will bombed the test.

jokes.text: Hold it, the 91 must be my percentile. But then, that means that.... I'm the most intelligent guy in the world.
jokes.setup: Will realizing his score interpretation was wrong
jokes.punchline: Will's ego immediately inflating again
jokes.explanation: Good reversal showing Will's quick recovery and ego. The dramatic pause builds the moment well.

jokes.text: You know, Carlton, you're gonna love driving those big rigs, man.
jokes.setup: Carlton realizing he scored lower than Will
jokes.punchline: Will predicting Carlton's blue-collar future
jokes.explanation: Good reversal of Carlton's earlier insult. The truck driving prediction is perfect contrast to Carlton's pretensions.

jokes.text: You can have my job, Master Carlton.
jokes.setup: Carlton's test score disappointment
jokes.punchline: Geoffrey offering his butler position to Carlton
jokes.explanation: Geoffrey's dry humor about Carlton's reduced prospects. Good economy and character voice.

jokes.text: Somewhere there's an Asian Will Smith and he's really ticked.
jokes.setup: Carlton's disbelief at Will's higher score
jokes.punchline: Meta-joke about Asian academic stereotypes
jokes.explanation: Clever meta-joke that plays with racial stereotypes and the actor's real name. Quotable and original.

jokes.text: This a window, this is Windex. Can you say that, little boy?
jokes.setup: Carlton catching Will's mistake
jokes.punchline: Will's desperate attempt to maintain superiority
jokes.explanation: Will's stubborn refusal to admit error. The condescending tone while being wrong creates cringe humor.

jokes.text: And fortunately you're too young to know what he was waving.
jokes.setup: Geoffrey giving a 'sad little wave' when he quit
jokes.punchline: Implying Geoffrey gave them the finger
jokes.explanation: Unexpectedly edgy humor from Uncle Phil. Good misdirection from 'sad wave' to middle finger implication.

jokes.text: You'd be surprised how fast you can eat a sponge cake when you have to.
jokes.setup: Hilary almost getting thrown out of express line
jokes.punchline: Her solution of eating evidence
jokes.explanation: Perfectly absurd Hilary logic. The specific detail of sponge cake makes it funnier and more believable.

jokes.text: What do you think? It's like 91? Or a measly little 90?
jokes.setup: Commenting on the temperature
jokes.punchline: Using PSAT score numbers to needle Carlton
jokes.explanation: Clever callback using temperature to continue mocking Carlton. Good integration of callback into natural dialogue.

jokes.text: About what? About $91?
jokes.setup: Carlton wanting to bet on computer error
jokes.punchline: Will using the score number as bet amount
jokes.explanation: Good callback that works naturally in context. Will finding every opportunity to work in the number.

jokes.text: My... everything is so pink.
jokes.setup: Ashley helping with laundry
jokes.punchline: Ashley ruined all the clothes by washing colors together
jokes.explanation: Standard laundry disaster gag. Predictable but serviceable visual humor.

jokes.text: I know what you're implying. You don't think I can cut the mustard, do you?
jokes.setup: Asked to put mustard in refrigerator
jokes.punchline: Carlton taking idiom literally and being paranoid
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Carlton's paranoid state. The literal interpretation of the idiom fits his pedantic nature.

jokes.text: Maybe I have hit a little slump, but I'm sure even Albert Einstein had a bad day. God knows he never combed his hair.
jokes.setup: Carlton comparing himself to Einstein
jokes.punchline: Focusing on Einstein's hair instead of his genius
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Carlton's priorities and deflection. The hair observation is oddly specific and funny.

jokes.text: But I'm not adverse to sitting up and begging.
jokes.setup: Uncle Phil saying he doesn't get on his knees and beg
jokes.punchline: Offering to sit and beg like a dog instead
jokes.explanation: Good subversion of Uncle Phil's dignity. The dog imagery is unexpectedly self-deprecating for his character.

jokes.text: Of course, yours have a lot shorter distance to travel.
jokes.setup: Carlton claiming he got his shorts on faster
jokes.punchline: Will mocking Carlton's height
jokes.explanation: Classic height joke with good setup and economy. Expected but well-executed.

jokes.text: That's why they call it a pop quiz, Banks.
jokes.setup: Carlton's complaint about not studying
jokes.punchline: Coach's matter-of-fact explanation
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective deadpan response to Carlton's anxiety. Good economy of words.

jokes.text: I'll be subjected to over-crowding, bad food... and daily threats of personal violence. I'm not talking about prison. I'm talking about public school.
jokes.setup: Carlton describing terrible conditions expecting prison
jokes.punchline: Revealing he's talking about public school
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection joke with perfect Carlton snobbery. The structure builds perfectly to the punchline reveal.

jokes.text: Carlton, I think it's time for you to come out of the closet.
jokes.setup: Coach addressing Carlton's cheating behavior
jokes.punchline: Literal closet reference that sounds like coming out gay
jokes.explanation: Double entendre that works in context but creates awkward implications for 1990s TV.

jokes.text: Coach, are you familiar with the sports injury... cerebral-spinal-refrictionation?
jokes.setup: Will needs to explain Carlton's neck craning
jokes.punchline: Making up a medical-sounding condition
jokes.explanation: Brilliant fake medical term that sounds plausible. Will's quick thinking creates an elaborate con.

jokes.text: I mean, as you can see, he looks like a complete idiot.
jokes.setup: Will helping Carlton by explaining his 'condition'
jokes.punchline: Will insulting Carlton while helping him
jokes.explanation: Perfect Will move - helping Carlton while still getting in an insult. Great character consistency.

jokes.text: Did I say game? I meant the crisis in the Soviet Union.
jokes.setup: Coach admitting he was listening to the game instead of watching class
jokes.punchline: Awkward attempt to sound more serious/professional
jokes.explanation: Coach's transparent attempt to cover his unprofessionalism with current events reference.

jokes.text: There I was in the middle of an arbitration between two of my biggest clients... and I get pulled out because Butch Cassidy and the Sundance Kid here... want to pull a fast one.
jokes.setup: Uncle Phil being called away from important work
jokes.punchline: Comparing Will and Carlton to famous outlaws
jokes.explanation: Good reference that fits Uncle Phil's dramatic style. The outlaw comparison is apt for their scheme.

jokes.text: Be quiet. Don't even look at me. Look at me when I'm talking to you!
jokes.setup: Uncle Phil's parental anger
jokes.punchline: Contradictory commands in quick succession
jokes.explanation: Perfect angry parent contradiction. Shows Uncle Phil's flustered state through conflicting demands.

jokes.text: I might as well tell you right now... I won't come back for a penny less than $80 more per week. We'll give you $100 a week.
jokes.setup: Geoffrey demanding $80 more
jokes.punchline: Uncle Phil immediately offering even more
jokes.explanation: Good reversal showing how desperately they need Geoffrey back. Uncle Phil's quick capitulation is funny.

jokes.text: Unless you'd be willing to consider a full-time cook... preferably Jamaican, with an incredible set of... cookware.
jokes.setup: Geoffrey making additional requests
jokes.punchline: The pause before 'cookware' implies he was thinking something else
jokes.explanation: Mild double entendre with the pause creating the implication. Geoffrey pushing his luck after success.

jokes.text: Let's get one thing straight here, I wear the cookware in this house.
jokes.setup: Discussion of Jamaican cook with 'incredible cookware'
jokes.punchline: Aunt Viv asserting dominance with 'wear the pants' variation
jokes.explanation: Clever variation on 'wear the pants' idiom. Good callback to Geoffrey's double entendre while asserting Aunt Viv's authority.

jokes.text: I was about to jet to the Riviera with Whitney Houston... but since I'm grounded, I guess I can spare five or six months.
jokes.setup: Carlton asking if Will is busy
jokes.punchline: Will's sarcastic description of his exciting grounded life
jokes.explanation: Good Will sarcasm with specific celebrity reference that fits the era. Shows his attitude about punishment.

jokes.text: In fact, I would have gloated better than you did. Ten times better.
jokes.setup: Carlton admitting Will had a right to gloat
jokes.punchline: Carlton being competitive about gloating itself
jokes.explanation: Perfect Carlton moment - being competitive about literally everything, even losing gracefully. Excellent character writing.

jokes.text: Articulate, eloquent, erudite? No, wordy.
jokes.setup: Carlton offering sophisticated synonyms for his verbal skills
jokes.punchline: Will's blunt, simple assessment
jokes.explanation: Perfect contrast between Carlton's verbose style and Will's directness. Great structure with the list building to simple deflation.

jokes.text: and when it comes to personal hygiene, God knows, there's no comparison.
jokes.setup: Carlton listing all his superior qualities
jokes.punchline: Ending on personal hygiene as the ultimate insult to Will
jokes.explanation: Good escalation ending with personal attack. Carlton's pretentious delivery makes it funnier.

jokes.text: You irritate the hell out of me! Now, get out of my room!
jokes.setup: Carlton asking what else he does great
jokes.punchline: Will's exasperated honest answer
jokes.explanation: Good payoff to the scene's building tension. Will's honest explosion is cathartic and funny.

tmdb_overview: Will is a picture of confidence, while Carlton is a frazzled bundle of nerves preparing for college aptitude tests.
